explanation_md: Each `value` function lives in its own namespace. `a::value()` returns 10 and `b::value()` returns 20. The `::` operator selects the namespace, so there is no ambiguity and both print on separate lines.

prompt_md: Why do C++ programs write `std::cout` instead of just `cout`?

options.a: `std::` tells the compiler to use the slow, safe output path.
options.b: `cout` lives inside the `std` namespace; `std::` is the scope-resolution operator that identifies which namespace's `cout` to use.
options.c: `std::cout` is required by the C++20 standard; older standards allowed plain `cout`.
options.d: The `std::` prefix tells the compiler to include the `<iostream>` header automatically.

explanation_md: `std` is the namespace where the C++ Standard Library places all its names. Without `std::`, the compiler looks in the global namespace, where `cout` does not exist by default. The scope-resolution operator `::` says 'look inside `std` for the name `cout`'.

options.a: `using namespace std;` pulls `std::add` into scope but does not make `utils::add` visible; the call finds no matching function.
options.b: The programmer forgot to qualify the call; `add(3, 4)` calls no function because `utils::add` is not in scope, causing a compile error.
options.c: The call resolves to `utils::add` correctly; the output is 9 as intended.
options.d: `using namespace std;` is illegal when a namespace named `utils` is also defined.

explanation_md: `utils::add` is only visible as `utils::add` or inside the `utils` namespace block. `using namespace std;` brings `std` names into scope but does nothing for `utils`. The unqualified call `add(3, 4)` will fail to compile (no `add` in global or `std` scope), or if there were a `std::add` it would call the wrong one. The fix is `utils::add(3, 4)`.
`math::square(4)` returns `4 * 4 = 16`. `math::square(0)` returns `0 * 0 = 0`. Both are printed on separate lines.

prompt_md: What is the main risk of writing `using namespace std;` at the top of a large source file?

options.a: It causes every `std` function call to be twice as slow.
options.b: It prevents you from defining any functions in the same file.
options.c: Names you define (like `count` or `sort`) may silently collide with `std` names, causing ambiguity errors or unexpected function calls.
options.d: It is illegal in C++20 and will produce a compiler error.

explanation_md: `using namespace std;` pulls all of `std`'s names into the current scope. If you then define a function with the same name as a standard one (like `std::count` or `std::sort`), the compiler may report an ambiguity error or silently call the wrong function. Fully qualifying names (`std::cout`) is always unambiguous and is the preferred style in non-trivial code.
